Q1: I tried to keep a somewhat consistent study routine, usually setting aside time in the evenings after my part-time work. I'm a visual learner, so I'd organize my materials with color-coded notes and diagrams. With limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when I could access the internet and download things. I aimed for about two hours a day across the week, but honestly, it didn't always work out that way. I kept a planner to track what needed to be done, but sometimes life just got in the way.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I wanted to be consistent, but between work and other responsibilities, I often ended up cramming closer to deadlines. Some weeks I'd be really on top of things, logging in regularly and watching videos. Other weeks, especially when I was stressed about money or exhausted from work, I'd fall behind and have to catch up all at once. I tried my best to maintain some regularity, but it was hard.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if there were new announcements or materials, or because an assignment deadline was approaching. The gamified tasks actually motivated me to log in more often because I liked seeing my progress and earning those little badges. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if I'd moved up on the leaderboard or completed a challenge. Notifications on my phone also reminded me to check in when I might have otherwise forgotten.
Q4: Yes, after a few weeks the gamified elements really helped make logging in feel more natural. I started checking the LMS most mornings just to see my progress stats and what tasks I could complete. It became almost like checking social media - a quick habit. The badges and points gave me small wins that kept me coming back even when I wasn't feeling super motivated about the actual coursework.
Q5: The video lectures were most useful for me since I learn best visually. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and visual explanations. The gamified task system also helped because it broke things into smaller, manageable pieces with immediate feedback. I appreciated discussion boards too because seeing how other students approached problems helped me understand concepts differently. The visual summaries and infographics posted by the instructor were particularly helpful.
Q6: I often skipped the longer reading materials, especially dense PDFs without many visuals. They just didn't stick for me the way videos did, and with limited time, I prioritized what felt more efficient. Some of the optional practice quizzes I ignored when I was overwhelmed with required work. I also didn't engage much with some of the supplementary links to external websites because my internet access isn't always reliable and I couldn't afford the data.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see clear progress or understand how it connected to assignments and exams. The gamified elements really helped with this - earning points made even boring tasks feel more rewarding. Visual content that explained concepts clearly was always worth it. If something was going to take a long time and I couldn't see how it would help my grade, I'd often skip it or save it for later.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford time-wise. Required ones I'd push through even when tired or unmotivated because I knew they affected my grade. With optional stuff, I'd think about whether the effort would pay off, and often decided my time was better spent on required work or catching up on things I'd fallen behind on. The gamified optional tasks were different though - those felt more like games than work, so I'd do them even when stressed.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded videos and PDFs whenever I could because my internet isn't always reliable at home. I'd watch lectures offline on my phone during my commute to work or during breaks. I also made handwritten notes with lots of diagrams and visual maps that I'd study from constantly. Sometimes I'd borrow textbooks from friends or find free resources online that explained things better than the course materials.
Q10: A lot of my studying happened with printed notes spread out on my bed, drawing concept maps and reviewing diagrams I'd made. I'd quiz myself using flashcards I created by hand. Sometimes I'd rewatch downloaded videos multiple times offline. I also studied with a classmate in person sometimes, going over material together. None of that would show up in the LMS at all, but it was real learning time.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty common for me. I'd download a video or PDF once, then study from it multiple times over several days without going back online. My LMS activity would show one quick access, but I might have spent hours with that material offline. It was more practical given my internet situation, and honestly I focused better without the distraction of being online.
Q12: Around midterms things got really hard. I was picking up extra shifts at work because I needed the money, and I was exhausted all the time. My attendance dropped and I fell behind on videos. The stress was building up and even though my anxiety stayed manageable, my motivation just tanked. I kept meaning to catch up but there was always something more urgent. The gamified tasks helped pull me back in a bit, but I never fully recovered that lost ground.
Q13: Definitely. When work was demanding or I was worried about bills, the LMS was the first thing to suffer. I'd log in with good intentions but feel too mentally drained to actually engage with the material. Sometimes I'd open a video and realize I'd zoned out for ten minutes. Fatigue made it hard to concentrate, and competing responsibilities meant I often had to choose between studying and other essential things.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd be logged in, scrolling through materials, but my mind was elsewhere. I'd have a video playing but be thinking about work or money stress. Sometimes I'd click through pages to feel like I was doing something productive, but nothing was really sinking in. The LMS would show I was active, but the quality of that engagement was pretty low.
Q15: I postponed exam preparation way too long, which is probably why my exam score was so poor. I knew it was important but the material felt overwhelming and I kept telling myself I'd get to it after finishing assignments. I also avoided some discussion posts because I felt like I didn't understand the material well enough to contribute meaningfully. Fear of looking stupid held me back sometimes.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to log in and work. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates. The gamified system helped spread things out a bit because daily challenges gave me smaller deadlines to meet. But for bigger assignments, I was definitely a last-minute person, which added to my stress.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback or good scores on gamified tasks made me want to engage more. But when I got poor feedback on assignments, it was discouraging and sometimes made me avoid the course for a few days. I appreciated detailed feedback when I got it, but sometimes the turnaround was slow and by then I'd moved on mentally. Quick feedback from the gamified quizzes was more motivating.
Q18: The course structure with weekly modules helped me know what to focus on. The gamified elements added structure that worked well for me - daily tasks, weekly challenges, progress tracking. But eight online courses is a lot to manage, and sometimes this course got lost in the shuffle. The structure was there, but I didn't always have the bandwidth to follow it properly.
Q19: The visual materials and gamified tasks made engagement easier - they matched how I learn and gave me motivation when I was struggling. The point system and badges genuinely helped. What made it harder was when there were dense text-heavy readings or when assignment instructions weren't clear. Also, some weeks had way more content than others, which made pacing difficult. More consistent workloads would have helped.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to understand material but struggling. They might watch videos multiple times, revisit readings, participate in discussions - all showing high activity - but still not grasp concepts well enough to complete assignments successfully. Or they might be dealing with personal issues that affect their ability to produce work even though they're putting in effort. It's not always about not trying.
Q21: That kind of describes me in some ways. My LMS activity was moderate, but I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show up. Someone might download everything once, study intensively from those materials, and perform decently without logging in much. Though in my case, my exam performance didn't reflect my effort, which is frustrating. But I think offline studying is more common than people realize.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when you're active, what you access, how often you log in. They can flag if someone disappears or stops engaging. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, personal circumstances affecting performance, and whether someone is actually learning versus just clicking through. They can't capture the mental and emotional side of being a student.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. It's wanting to understand, not just pass. For me, engagement also means feeling like I'm part of something - interacting with classmates, feeling connected to the course. The gamified elements helped create that feeling of active participation rather than passive consumption.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied from downloaded materials offline, or the hours I spent making visual notes and diagrams. It wouldn't show that I was working part-time to afford school, or that financial stress affected my focus. It wouldn't capture that despite my poor exam score, I actually learned things that matter to me. And it wouldn't show that the gamified tasks genuinely improved my mood and made me feel more capable, even when I was struggling.
